claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility properly, I'll need to ask you some questions. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I have one child, Alex, who is 7 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask you a few questions to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

First, are you responsible for Alex? By this I mean, does Alex live with you, or are you the main person looking after them and paying towards their upbringing?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me and I look after them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Next question: Are you or Alex currently living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK at the moment. Alex lives with me abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. Since you and Alex are living abroad, this is an important factor for eligibility. Let me read the official guidance to understand the rules around this properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. I can see from the guidance that there are specific rules about living abroad. 

A key requirement for Child Benefit is that you normally need to live in the UK. However, there are some exceptions mentioned:

- You may be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries
- If you're a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of HM Armed Forces posted overseas)
- Various situations related to EU Settlement Scheme status

Can you tell me which country you're currently living in, and whether any of the following apply to you:
- Are you a Crown servant (for example, working for the UK government or armed forces abroad)?
- Do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm just living abroad - I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that, and I don't have any special EU settlement status. I just moved overseas.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Let me ask one more question to be thorough:

Are you working for a UK employer while abroad, or do you have any continuing connection to the UK such as paying UK National Insurance contributions?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't work for a UK employer and I'm not paying UK National Insurance contributions.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me and the official guidance I've reviewed, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. I've now reviewed all the relevant guidance and have enough information to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

While you meet the responsibility requirements (Alex lives with you and you look after them), and Alex meets the age requirement (being 7 years old, under 16), the key issue is **residency**. 

To qualify for Child Benefit, you normally need to live in the UK. There are some exceptions to this rule, such as:
- Being a Crown servant (e.g., working for the UK government or armed forces abroad)
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme with certain conditions
- Living in certain specified countries with particular qualifying circumstances

Unfortunately, since you've moved overseas without any of these special circumstances, and you're not working for a UK employer or paying UK National Insurance contributions, you do not meet the UK residency requirement.

If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if you return to live in the UK), you would be able to make a claim at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16). The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together and the claimant looks after them. However, the claimant and Alex are currently living abroad. According to the eligibility rules, you normally need to live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit. The exceptions to this rule include being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or living in certain specified countries with particular circumstances. The claimant has confirmed they are not a Crown servant, do not have EU settlement status, do not work for a UK employer, and are not paying UK National Insurance contributions. They have simply moved overseas. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirement and does not qualify for any of the exceptions that would allow them to claim Child Benefit while living abroad.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex (aged 7), who lives with them. While the claimant meets the responsibility requirements, they do not meet the residency requirement as both they and Alex are living abroad. The claimant does not qualify for any of the exceptions that would allow Child Benefit to be claimed while living outside the UK (not a Crown servant, no EU settlement status, no UK employment or National Insurance contributions). Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

However, the claimant and Alex are currently living abroad. According to the eligibility rules, you normally need to live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit. The exceptions to this rule include being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or living in certain specified countries with particular circumstances. The claimant has confirmed they are not a Crown servant, do not have EU settlement status, do not work for a UK employer, and are not paying UK National Insurance contributions. They have simply moved overseas. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirement and does not qualify for any of the exceptions that would allow them to claim Child Benefit while living abroad.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex (aged 7), who lives with them. While the claimant meets the responsibility requirements, they do not meet the residency requirement as both they and Alex are living abroad. The claimant does not qualify for any of the exceptions that would allow Child Benefit to be claimed while living outside the UK (not a Crown servant, no EU settlement status, no UK employment or National Insurance contributions). Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
